author: Kr1gs
content: It's not just that Labs was a "test".  It was just pretty standard practice to not worry about compute limits or blockheight.  And even if people were adding priority fees, it was a single lamport only.  So I wouldn't put this on SA devs at all.  Nothing to do with pressure to release Labs imo.  Just standard industry practice.  Yeah, re: priority fees thats why Phantom is working better then Solflare right now.  They impose pretty high priority fees.  Where Solflare leaves it up to the Dapps.  So our prior recommendations for everyone to swap to Solflare as sort of turned on its end.  re: solana surprise... it was a long year at sub 20. people were building as efficinetly as they could and not thinking about token price.  Hell I'd say most devs arent thinking about token price anyways.   I also expect see rapid improvement on all facets in the next couple of months.  Novemember/December was a whole lot of fact finding on what the problems were and once all the teams are back from vacation i would expect this is the number #1 problem to fix.

author: ! SH | Bohemian ⪛⦿⫺
content: No.  Not everyone needs priority fees.  Some transactions are dependent on each other so they have to have to go first, e.g. priority fees are necessary.  So what happens if they fail repeatedly? The general answer is to just keep slamming.  That puts even more congestion on the network.  If we can sort out what actually requires priority and what doesn't then hopefully it will reduce congestion overall.   Bit of a self fulfilling prophecy. .    I think the other bit that will come from this is better error checking and handling.  Right now if we run into a block height error it just errors out the UI.  Hopefully in the future these types of errors will retry and catch more elegantly.

reply.preview: No. Not everyone needs priority fees. Some transactions are dependent on each other so they have to have to go first, e.g. priority fees are necessary. So what happens if they fail repeatedly? The general answer is to just keep slamming. That puts even more congestion on the network. If we can sort out what actually requires priority and what doesn't then hopefully it will reduce congestion overall. Bit of a self fulfilling prophecy. . I think the other bit that will come from this is better error checking and handling. Right now if we run into a block height error it just errors out the UI. Hopefully in the future these types of errors will retry and catch more elegantly.

author: ZiggyMUD
content:
One of my 2024 Predictions

#1 - Web3 phones (or physical products with free airdrops) will be an even bigger trend in 2024 than December 2023 and will bring new attention to lots of new projects that give users free airdrops with these products. Think of it as a new way of marketing.

Why do I think this?
Do you really notice a big difference in phone models from one year to the next. Could these free airdrops spur the new generation of growth in the product consumption markets kinda similar in how Netflix temporarily changed the business model for consuming media or how free-to-play gaming changed the gaming industry? I'm not saying these phones will be free, but there is the potential for them to be and even a chance for early adopters to make money (hence driving consumption demand)

Example - I won two Claynosaurz's shortly after buying the Solana SAGA phone and each one is now worth a minimum of $4,100 USD, possibly higher depending on characteristics.

I also got an airdrop of Bonk worth $1000 close to its peak price.

So with my Bonk and Claynosaurz prizes the SAGA phone has paid me over $8,000 USD over the purchase price on top of other benefits. (And I paid the full price at $1,000usd)

I was an early adopter at a time when prices for BONK and NFTs were at their lows. People should not expect to make the same but stories like this could lead to more phone sales, in turn creating enough users for more dapps to be built for the SAGA phone.

What are your thoughts?

Should the Star Atlas Crew app become a dapp for 25,000 new Saga users, who already have a Solana wallet and should be crypto native?
Happy New Year @Chri.z 
I would love to hear your thoughts on this idea & a few statements below .

author: 10^24 Stars
content:
I definitely see airdrops function as an advertisement mechanism and/or a reward to project core community.
Seems to me that SA could pull in more of the web3 gaming community by using a portion of the crew mint as a free campaign airdrop to collaborate with other gaming communities and pull more of the current web3 users into SA.  Theres nothing like "free" stuff to draw crowds.

But retention is definitely something to consider.... like 10-20% retention would probably be considered good in this space but that is probably well worth it.
I'm not a marketing person by any measure

author: 🅰🅲🅴🅺🅸🅽🅶MUD
content:
this is from GPT 4.0   .. mixed up commander and capital ... LOL

=

User
please tell me the difference between a Commander Ship and a Capital Ship in the blockchain game Star Atlas

ChatGPT
In the blockchain game Star Atlas, the difference between a "Commander Ship" and a "Capital Ship" primarily lies in their size, functionality, and role within the game.

Commander Ship:

Size and Structure: Commander Ships are typically smaller than Capital Ships. They are designed for agility and speed rather than sheer power.
Functionality and Role: These ships are often used for specific tasks such as exploration, skirmishes, and tactical missions. They are more suited for individual players or small groups, offering a balance between firepower, speed, and maneuverability.
Gameplay Impact: Commander Ships may offer more flexibility in gameplay, allowing players to undertake a variety of missions and explore different aspects of the Star Atlas universe.
Capital Ship:

Size and Structure: Capital Ships are among the largest in the game, equipped with formidable firepower and strong defenses. They are designed for sustained combat and can withstand heavy damage.
Functionality and Role: These ships serve as the flagship for larger fleets and are often used in major battles and territorial control. They can carry multiple smaller ships and provide support in large-scale conflicts.
Gameplay Impact: Capital Ships require a collaborative effort to operate effectively, often necessitating a coordinated team or guild. They play a crucial role in controlling sectors of space and in large-scale player-versus-player engagements.
In summary, Commander Ships are more versatile and agile, suited for individual or small group missions, while Capital Ships are larger, more powerful, and designed for large-scale conflicts and fleet operations. The choice between the two depends on the player's preferred style of gameplay and role within the Star Atlas universe

author: Sammy | stegaBOB [SKR]
content: that's one way to look at it.. other way is to keep main functions running smooth and keep a ratio on how much you rush into new things (v2 is a good/bad example). I was thinking your way bro, for a long time, gave it a couple of years of ease. As far i can see, everthing will still remain beta if we dont fix things. I am always giving huge kudos and slack to the hard working team and all management after all they went through.. the FTX thing, letgo's of the staff, the bear market, etc... but we went through that as well.. It's just, they had to clear out the tail winds on main issues, otherwise forget the main capital flow.. i mean, how can a serious investor can lock up, say a mere couple of hundred K USD to polis locker, if he has to retry for a full quarter of an NBA game

reply.preview: that's one way to look at it.. other way is to keep main functions running smooth and keep a ratio on how much you rush into new things (v2 is a good/bad example). author: Sammy | stegaBOB [SKR]
content: Reason why things have been taking longer than usual to fix recently is because most of the new issues are due to Solana itself. Network usage increased significantly which requires priority fees on a lot of transactions. Our codebase wasnt designed to handle that, so stuff broke.

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content:
I would argue that for an early project where on boarding is absolutely key, SA needs to own the onboarding process.

IRL, I work with the world's top CPG's (Nestle, Coca Cola, P&G, etc). For decades, their GTM has been to sell through distributors such as grocery stores. The challenge is that they are then leaving the end customer experience (CX) to 3rd parties. If those 3rd parties fked it up, it still reflects on the CPG.

In the last 2-3 years, these CPGs are investing billions in DTC (direct to consumer). Why? Because if the CX is bad, that has a big negative impact on the core CPG brand.

SA is similar; if the onboarding ramp is left to 3rd parties, that opens up a lot of risk to a crappy CX that reflects poorly onto SA. This is not something a nascent project can afford. SA needs to have a first party, owned path of seamless onboarding as a baseline. Others can build alternate paths sure, but it can't be left solely for 3rd parties to do.

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: The short answer is that we don't have bandwidth to build all of these things out, including something that appears as simple as "integrating into the marketplace." My philosophy on this is to enable partners to build and release as soon as possible so that the features are at least available. Every feature/release that isn't the core game development pushes that game development farther out. I'd rather not prioritize adding bundles to the marketplace when we can prioritize combat systems and starbase contruction + upgrading. And in that latter scenario, the Neoswap integration might not come out until second half of this year.   I personally don't put much weight into the "bad user onboarding" argument. I believe users understand that they are interacting with a third party for a particular service. Starpath/Buddylink is the same thing, btw. You link out to the Buddy dashboard to manage your profile and account. In that case, we actually provided them designs, so it's better, but we also have constraints on design. Frankly, I just don't care that much. Quantity over quality with respect to outside features.   Ultimately the whole vision is tailored to third party development across our ecosystem. We're not going to control every aspect of that, nor do we want to. The best analogy I can give is that of any layer 1. Solana doesn't influence what we build, or how it looks. And it is arguable that we are an entry point and user onboarding onto Solana. I know first hand we brought a lot of users to Solana for the first time in 2021.  Let the builders build. The core attraction to Star Atlas has nothing to do with Neoswap, Buddy, SLY tooling, Pitstop, AMMs, LPs, etc. It's the game. That's our focus. Everything else is a bonus.  edit: that ended up not being a short answer

author: [AEP] Mendizo
content:
@odvb it's definitely a bit of a conundrum at the moment; like Duncan said, I would agree that we may just need to advise them on the current reality. Assuming that this is specific to Labs (since in Showroom they can do racing and such), the current gameplay loops in labs (scanning for SDU's, mining, crafting) don't really provide for an immediate expected ROI for a single XXS ship. 

SA has multiple gameplay facets to it, and I don't think it necessarily is a negative that right now, certain facets make sense for certain players, and other facets don't make sense. For someone with a single XXS ship, they can enjoy the Showroom right now where they can get full 'use' out of their ship, and look to the near future in Labs. When things like combat, renting, and especially player contracts come out, that is a lot more variety. We just of course don't want to make people feel small or insignificant in this response, but I do think folks appreciate a real, transparent FYI on this upfront.

author: Matt | IntergalacticHerald
content:
I agreed to set expectations that SAGE needs larger fleets to really get into the economy aspects.  But, there are other ways to "play" Star Atlas with 1 or 2 small ships.-The first is to just own part of Star Atlas.  By buying a ship, they are part of the Star Atlas metaverse and community right now.  If they are interested the type of gameplay that Star Atlas is developing (sci-fi, exploration, combat, politics, etc), then why not buy a ship now.
-The second is that, if they have a PC with a decent video card, they can be part of the UE5 game development and interact with the Showroom right now.  They can start learning the racing module which seems to be focused on the small racer ships.  They can explore the Showroom until the racing is live.
-The third is SCORE.  They won't get a lot of ATLAS rewards with a small ship but they can start to see how the economy works.
-Finally, they could try to play SAGE but again, there really isn't a lot a couple of small ships can do.

The most important thing to me is to tell the small ship gamers that Star Atlas is a game in development and they get a chance to be involved in the development of a new blockchain-based game now.  The traditional gamer experience of a $60 console game like Starfield isn't what Star Atlas is at the present time.  If that is what they want to do, suggest to check back with Star Atlas every 6 months and see what is happening then.  Or, stick around and maybe buy another ship in a couple of months.  Build up a fleet in a year and then get into SAGE or whatever SAGE is like at that point.  If you want to see a game being development, Star Atlas is a great place to join the community.

@Barre WRT your question: yes, StarComm is being completely rebuild. One of the big changes is that they are moving from JavaScript to Rust. This alone should bring drastic performance improvements. They of course also learned a lot, so all those lessons learned are going to be incorporated as well.

Given the importance of this middle-layer (and headache is causes atm), I think this is an excellent idea

author: [AEP] Funcracker [AEP]
content: Also, I would not be surprised if they will rebuild it again in a few years, as the ecosystem is changing rapidly, and as they bump into the next issues when DAU scales up a 2+ orders of magnitude. But let's see

author: bunthius [STAR]
content: @bunthius I'm aware that the CREW will have the levels (common to anomaly)... I'm assuming crew have a mix of job functions as well?

reply.preview: @bunthius I'm aware that the CREW will have the levels (common to anomaly)... I'm assuming crew have a mix of job functions as well?

author: 𝐑𝐎𝐌𝐄 | Virtuwul [ROME]
content: Yes, basically correct. The levels/rarity applies to how well they perform when assigned to specific job functions. There are 7 aptitudes each crew has that at the moment apply to 14 different job functions. Based on the rarity level they get a certain amount of aptidues and different buffs on the rolled aptitude should they be assigned to that role. If you roll a FLIGHT aptitude that buffs the xp gain in the Navigator or Pilot seats. So on and so on. And the aptitude being a Major, Minor or Anomalous rating also applies to the xp gain buff rate. I'm sure we'll have a chart detailing all this once it's totally locked down and releasable.

content: Here's a suggestion for an interesting game mode...  You spawn in a C11 or TreeArrow with a team of 4-6 players and you have a mission to accomplish (go get X item) with a 15 minute timer and the ship is loaded with creatures.  You could do a Resurgence game mode.  If a team member dies, they respawn in 30 seconds.  If the entire team gets wiped or if the timer runs out, you lose.
